title: Subqueries, CTEs, Set Operations, and Relational Reasoning · Result-Grain Case: Recursive Ctes
summary: Operations needs reusable query stages for backlog comparisons, missing relationships, and cohorts across time windows. Planned artifact: Add and verify the sql recursive ctes increment in a composable backlog-analysis query pipeline. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Define anchor, recursive step, termination, and cycle or depth safeguards for hierarchical queries.
Use CTEs to name query stages while distinguishing readability from materialization guarantees.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: Compare each ticket with its team's effort and severity peers, retain teams with no resolved work, and rank ties deliberately. Build named query stages, one changed window of 6 rows, and a reconciliation total. Competency probe: Define anchor, recursive step, termination, and cycle or depth safeguards for hierarchical queries. A defensible solution applies Recursive Ctes, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to a composable backlog-analysis query pipeline.

content.code:
-- Worked focus: sql-recursive-ctes — Define anchor, recursive step, termination, and cycle or depth safeguards for hierarchical queries.
WITH team_counts AS (
  SELECT team_id, COUNT(*) AS ticket_count
  FROM tickets
  GROUP BY team_id
)
SELECT teams.name, COALESCE(team_counts.ticket_count, 0) AS ticket_count
FROM teams
LEFT JOIN team_counts USING (team_id)
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM tickets
  WHERE tickets.team_id = teams.team_id AND status = 'investigating'
)
ORDER BY teams.name;
content.caption: Runnable relational query or data-system contract example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

content.code:
-- Evidence: sql-recursive-ctes-gd2bfe
-- grain: declare the exact row or mutation grain before running
WITH unresolved AS (
  SELECT ticket_id, team_id FROM tickets WHERE status <> 'resolved'
)
SELECT team_id, COUNT(*) AS unresolved_count
FROM unresolved GROUP BY team_id ORDER BY team_id;
-- observation: reconcile the returned rows, changed count, error, or plan with the prediction
